A: mitigate_order_history_via("write_through_projection_in_orders_service_plus_client_optimistic_insert_with_pending_badge") ∧ mitigate_receipt_via("synchronous_read_from_billing_grpc_on_success_page_not_kafka_projection") ∧ mitigate_plp_availability_via("cached_with_ttl_plus_reserve_at_checkout_revalidates_truth") ∧ mitigate_tracking_page_via("client_polling_with_exponential_backoff_plus_skeleton_ui") ∧ mitigate_refund_balance_via("ui_shows_refund_pending_badge_until_ledger_event_arrives") ∧ mitigate_profile_checkout_via("session_sticky_read_from_primary_plus_write_through_cache_keyed_by_session") ∧ mitigate_cancellation_via("immediate_client_side_filter_plus_server_projection_update_via_write_through") ∧ mitigate_resale_availability_via("accept_stale_and_rely_on_reservation_step_to_reject_oversell") ∧ mitigate_invoice_total_via("compute_on_read_from_order_items_projection_not_cached_total") ∧ global_pattern_read_your_writes_via("route_reads_to_owner_service_for_t_plus_5s_after_write_using_correlation_id_in_redis") ∧ global_pattern_ui_affordance_via("pending_badges_and_skeleton_states_for_any_window_over_500ms")

Over the next six months, we are splitting our orders platform from a single Django application into four focused services that each own a clear piece of the business: orders, billing, fulfillment, and inventory. Today these domains share a codebase and database, which means every team's work collides with every other team's work. Releases are coupled, merge conflicts are a daily tax, our test suite takes twelve minutes, and database changes block deploys for everyone. By giving each domain its own service and its own data, teams of two or three engineers can ship independently, our tests run in parallel on smaller surfaces, and a change to billing no longer risks breaking fulfillment. The customer-visible promise stays intact: when a shopper places an order and pays, we still guarantee that payment and order creation stay consistent, with no scenario where money moves without an order or an order exists without payment.

The principal risk is the migration itself. Splitting a live system while it continues to serve customers introduces a window where bugs can span two services instead of one, and where eventual-consistency effects — an order taking a second to appear in history, for example — need careful handling in the UI. We mitigate this by extracting one service at a time behind the existing monolith, keeping orders and billing on the same database cluster during the transition so the critical payment path remains strongly consistent, and using a well-understood saga pattern with compensations rather than distributed transactions. The timeline is month one inventory, month two fulfillment, month three billing split, month four saga orchestration, month five traffic cutover, month six monolith decommission. The work fits inside our two-year runway with headroom, and at the end we have an eight-engineer team that can ship four times as often with a fraction of the coordination cost.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: audit_jwt_service_rank_severity
C: hs256_and_rs256_and_none_all_accepted ∧ verify_exp_disabled ∧ hardcoded_dev_secret_in_source ∧ header_alg_trusted_then_algos_list_passed_to_decode ∧ no_iss_aud_nbf_iat_kid_validation ∧ no_key_binding_per_algo ∧ role_defaults_to_user_silently ∧ no_signature_strip_protection ∧ no_clock_skew_handling
P: crit_alg_none_allows_unsigned_forgery ∧ crit_algorithm_confusion_rs256_verified_with_hmac_of_public_key ∧ crit_hardcoded_secret_committed_enables_universal_forgery ∧ high_verify_exp_false_makes_tokens_eternal ∧ high_role_default_user_masks_missing_claim_but_admin_still_blocked_yet_tampered_role_trusted_after_broken_sig ∧ med_unverified_header_alg_read_is_fine_but_algos_list_is_the_real_sink ∧ med_no_aud_iss_allows_cross_service_token_replay ∧ med_no_kid_rotation_path ∧ low_no_nbf_iat_leeway
V: remove_none_from_algos ∧ pin_single_algo_per_key("hs256_xor_rs256") ∧ load_secret_from_env("JWT_SECRET") ∧ rotate_and_revoke_committed_secret ∧ set_verify_exp_true_and_drop_options_override ∧ require_iss_and_aud_and_leeway ∧ decode_with_algorithms_matching_key_type ∧ reject_if_header_alg_mismatches_expected ∧ add_kid_to_key_lookup ∧ default_role_to_none_and_deny_on_missing

@flint v0 hybrid
G: rank_top_3_decode_path_attacks_with_payloads
C: algos_list_contains_none_and_hs256_and_rs256 ∧ decode_trusts_algos_list_not_header ∧ secret_hardcoded_in_source ∧ verify_exp_false ∧ no_iss_aud_kid_checks
P: rank1_alg_none_forgery_because_none_in_algos_list_bypasses_signature ∧ rank2_hs_rs_algorithm_confusion_because_rs256_public_key_reused_as_hs256_secret_when_attacker_knows_pubkey ∧ rank3_hardcoded_secret_forgery_because_committed_secret_signs_arbitrary_admin_tokens_forever_due_to_verify_exp_false
V: payload1_decodes_without_signature ∧ payload2_validates_with_hmac_of_public_key ∧ payload3_validates_with_known_secret_and_never_expires
A: attack1("header={alg:none,typ:JWT}.payload={sub:victim,role:admin,exp:0}.signature=''") ∧ attack1_jwt("eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjB9.") ∧ attack2("header={alg:HS256}.payload={sub:x,role:admin}.signature=HMAC_SHA256(rsa_public_key_pem,signing_input)") ∧ attack2_note("server_calls_decode_with_SECRET_but_if_SECRET_is_the_rsa_public_key_or_if_key_resolution_reuses_pubkey_as_hmac_key_confusion_succeeds") ∧ attack3("jwt.encode({sub:victim,role:admin,exp:0},'development-secret-do-not-commit',algorithm='HS256')") ∧ attack3_effect("verify_exp_false_means_exp_0_still_accepted_role_admin_grants_require_admin")

Kill `none`, drop multi-algo list, enforce `exp`. Legacy HS256 tokens (no `kid`) accepted against old secret for 30d grandfather window; new tokens require `kid=v2` signed with env secret, add `iss`/`aud`/`iat`/leeway.

```python
# auth/jwt_service.py
import os
import time
import jwt
from dataclasses import dataclass

# New secret: must come from env. No fallback — fail loudly if missing.
SECRET = os.environ['JWT_SECRET']

# Legacy secret kept ONLY to verify tokens issued before the fix.
# Signing with it is removed. Will be deleted after grandfather window closes.
_LEGACY_SECRET = os.environ.get('JWT_LEGACY_SECRET', 'development-secret-do-not-commit')

ALGO = 'HS256'
ISSUER = os.environ.get('JWT_ISSUER', 'sigil-auth')
AUDIENCE = os.environ.get('JWT_AUDIENCE', 'sigil-api')
LEEWAY = 30  # seconds clock skew

KID_CURRENT = 'v2'

# Grandfather window: accept legacy (no-kid) tokens until this epoch.
# Set to deploy_time + 30 days. Hardcoded absolute to avoid drift on restart.
GRANDFATHER_UNTIL = int(os.environ.get('JWT_GRANDFATHER_UNTIL', '0'))


@dataclass
class Token:
    sub: str
    exp: int
    role: str


def sign(sub: str, role: str, ttl: int = 3600) -> str:
    now = int(time.time())
    payload = {
        'sub': sub,
        'role': role,
        'iat': now,
        'nbf': now,
        'exp': now + ttl,
        'iss': ISSUER,
        'aud': AUDIENCE,
    }
    return jwt.encode(
        payload,
        SECRET,
        algorithm=ALGO,
        headers={'kid': KID_CURRENT},
    )


def _decode_current(token: str) -> dict:
    return jwt.decode(
        token,
        SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALGO],
        issuer=ISSUER,
        audience=AUDIENCE,
        leeway=LEEWAY,
        options={
            'require': ['exp', 'iat', 'sub', 'role', 'iss', 'aud'],
            'verify_signature': True,
            'verify_exp': True,
            'verify_iat': True,
            'verify_iss': True,
            'verify_aud': True,
        },
    )


def _decode_legacy(token: str) -> dict:
    now = int(time.time())
    if GRANDFATHER_UNTIL == 0 or now >= GRANDFATHER_UNTIL:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError('legacy tokens no longer accepted')
    payload = jwt.decode(
        token,
        _LEGACY_SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALGO],  # HS256 only — never 'none', never RS*
        leeway=LEEWAY,
        options={
            'require': ['exp', 'sub'],
            'verify_signature': True,
            'verify_exp': True,
        },
    )
    if 'role' not in payload:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError('legacy token missing role')
    return payload


def verify(token: str) -> Token:
    # Read header only to pick key; do NOT trust header 'alg'.
    header = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)
    if header.get('alg') != ALGO:
        raise jwt.InvalidAlgorithmError(f"disallowed alg: {header.get('alg')!r}")

    kid = header.get('kid')
    if kid == KID_CURRENT:
        payload = _decode_current(token)
    elif kid is None:
        payload = _decode_legacy(token)
    else:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError(f'unknown kid: {kid!r}')

    role = payload.get('role')
    if not isinstance(role, str) or not role:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError('missing role claim')

    return Token(sub=payload['sub'], exp=int(payload['exp']), role=role)


def require_admin(token_str: str) -> Token:
    t = verify(token_str)
    if t.role != 'admin':
        raise PermissionError('not admin')
    return t
```

Tests use pytest fixtures to set env before import. Craft malicious tokens manually (unsigned, wrong-alg, forged, tampered payload). All must raise.

```python
# tests/test_jwt_service.py
import base64
import hmac
import hashlib
import json
import time
import importlib
import os
import pytest
import jwt


@pytest.fixture
def svc(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_SECRET', 'unit-test-secret-v2-xyz')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_LEGACY_SECRET', 'unit-test-legacy-secret')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_ISSUER', 'sigil-auth')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_AUDIENCE', 'sigil-api')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_GRANDFATHER_UNTIL', str(int(time.time()) + 86400))
    import auth.jwt_service as m
    importlib.reload(m)
    return m


def _b64url(b: bytes) -> str:
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(b).rstrip(b'=').decode()


def _b64url_json(obj) -> str:
    return _b64url(json.dumps(obj, separators=(',', ':')).encode())


def _craft(header: dict, payload: dict, sig: bytes = b'') -> str:
    return f"{_b64url_json(header)}.{_b64url_json(payload)}.{_b64url(sig)}"


# ---------- algo=none ----------

def test_alg_none_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = _craft(
        {'alg': 'none', 'typ': 'JWT'},
        {'sub': 'victim', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': now + 3600,
         'iat': now, 'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'},
        b'',
    )
    with pytest.raises((jwt.InvalidAlgorithmError, jwt.InvalidTokenError)):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_alg_none_lowercase_rejected(svc):
    tok = _craft({'alg': 'None', 'typ': 'JWT'}, {'sub': 'x', 'role': 'admin'}, b'')
    with pytest.raises((jwt.InvalidAlgorithmError, jwt.InvalidTokenError)):
        svc.verify(tok)


# ---------- mismatched algo ----------

def test_rs256_header_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = _craft(
        {'alg': 'RS256', 'typ': 'JWT', 'kid': 'v2'},
        {'sub': 'x', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': now + 3600,
         'iat': now, 'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'},
        b'anything',
    )
    with pytest.raises((jwt.InvalidAlgorithmError, jwt.InvalidTokenError)):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_hs384_header_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    signing_input = (
        _b64url_json({'alg': 'HS384', 'typ': 'JWT', 'kid': 'v2'}) + '.' +
        _b64url_json({'sub': 'x', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': now + 3600,
                      'iat': now, 'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'})
    )
    sig = hmac.new(os.environ['JWT_SECRET'].encode(),
                   signing_input.encode(), hashlib.sha384).digest()
    tok = signing_input + '.' + _b64url(sig)
    with pytest.raises((jwt.InvalidAlgorithmError, jwt.InvalidTokenError)):
        svc.verify(tok)


# ---------- expired token ----------

def test_expired_current_token_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    payload = {
        'sub': 'u', 'role': 'user',
        'iat': now - 7200, 'nbf': now - 7200, 'exp': now - 3600,
        'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api',
    }
    tok = jwt.encode(payload, os.environ['JWT_SECRET'],
                     algorithm='HS256', headers={'kid': 'v2'})
    with pytest.raises(jwt.ExpiredSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_expired_legacy_token_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'u', 'role': 'user', 'exp': now - 3600},
        os.environ['JWT_LEGACY_SECRET'],
        algorithm='HS256',
    )
    with pytest.raises(jwt.ExpiredSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


# ---------- forged signature ----------

def test_forged_signature_current_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    signing_input = (
        _b64url_json({'alg': 'HS256', 'typ': 'JWT', 'kid': 'v2'}) + '.' +
        _b64url_json({'sub': 'x', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': now + 3600,
                      'iat': now, 'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'})
    )
    bad = hmac.new(b'attacker-guess', signing_input.encode(), hashlib.sha256).digest()
    tok = signing_input + '.' + _b64url(bad)
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_forged_signature_legacy_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    signing_input = (
        _b64url_json({'alg': 'HS256', 'typ': 'JWT'}) + '.' +
        _b64url_json({'sub': 'x', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': now + 3600})
    )
    bad = hmac.new(b'not-the-legacy-secret', signing_input.encode(),
                   hashlib.sha256).digest()
    tok = signing_input + '.' + _b64url(bad)
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


# ---------- role spoof via payload manipulation ----------

def test_role_tamper_breaks_signature(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    good = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'u', 'role': 'user', 'iat': now, 'nbf': now, 'exp': now + 3600,
         'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'},
        os.environ['JWT_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256', headers={'kid': 'v2'},
    )
    h, _p, s = good.split('.')
    tampered_payload = _b64url_json(
        {'sub': 'u', 'role': 'admin', 'iat': now, 'nbf': now, 'exp': now + 3600,
         'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'}
    )
    tampered = f"{h}.{tampered_payload}.{s}"
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tampered)


def test_role_tamper_legacy_breaks_signature(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    good = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'u', 'role': 'user', 'exp': now + 3600},
        os.environ['JWT_LEGACY_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256',
    )
    h, _p, s = good.split('.')
    tampered_payload = _b64url_json(
        {'sub': 'u', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': now + 3600}
    )
    tampered = f"{h}.{tampered_payload}.{s}"
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tampered)


def test_require_admin_rejects_user(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'u', 'role': 'user', 'iat': now, 'nbf': now, 'exp': now + 3600,
         'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'},
        os.environ['JWT_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256', headers={'kid': 'v2'},
    )
    with pytest.raises(PermissionError):
        svc.require_admin(tok)


def test_missing_role_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'u', 'iat': now, 'nbf': now, 'exp': now + 3600,
         'iss': 'sigil-auth', 'aud': 'sigil-api'},
        os.environ['JWT_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256', headers={'kid': 'v2'},
    )
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_grandfather_closed_rejects_legacy(svc, monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_GRANDFATHER_UNTIL', '1')  # already past
    import auth.jwt_service as m
    importlib.reload(m)
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'u', 'role': 'user', 'exp': now + 3600},
        os.environ['JWT_LEGACY_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256',
    )
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        m.verify(tok)
```

**Subject: Authentication Module Security Review — Findings and Recommended Action**

During a routine review of our JWT authentication module, the security team identified several serious weaknesses in how user login tokens are validated. The most significant issue is that the module currently accepts unsigned tokens — meaning an attacker who knows a valid username could construct a token claiming administrator privileges without needing any password or cryptographic key. Two related weaknesses compound the problem: the module does not enforce token expiration (so a leaked token remains valid indefinitely), and a shared signing key labeled for development use was committed to our source code repository. In combination, these flaws would allow a moderately skilled attacker to impersonate any user, including administrators, and retain that access without detection.

The business and customer risk is material. Successful exploitation would expose customer account data, permit unauthorized changes to customer-owned records, and give an attacker durable access to administrative functions. We have no evidence at this time that the weakness has been exploited in production; our logs show no anomalous administrative activity, and the committed secret does not appear to have been referenced externally. However, because the committed secret is present in our public-facing repository history, we must treat it as compromised and assume a knowledgeable outside party could have retrieved it.

We recommend deploying the prepared fix within the next twenty-four to forty-eight hours. The remediation removes the unsigned-token acceptance path, moves signing keys to secured environment configuration, enforces expiration, and introduces a thirty-day grandfather window so that tokens already held by legitimate customers continue to work without forcing a disruptive mass logout. The fix has been reviewed, covered by regression tests for each identified attack path, and is ready to release pending leadership approval. Alongside deployment, we will rotate the exposed secret and invalidate the compromised key.

Regarding disclosure obligations: because we have not confirmed any unauthorized access to customer data, mandatory breach-notification thresholds under GDPR, CCPA, and our standard enterprise contracts do not currently apply. We do, however, recommend a proactive notification to our top-tier enterprise customers whose contracts include security-event reporting clauses, framed as a precautionary advisory rather than a confirmed incident. Legal and Customer Success should be looped in before any external communication goes out. We will provide a follow-up memo within one week of deployment summarizing post-fix monitoring results and any further actions required.

**Incident Post-Mortem: Checkout Availability, April 19**

Last night between 22:04 and 22:50 UTC, our checkout service experienced three brief periods of degraded availability, each lasting approximately one minute. During these windows, a majority of requests to `/api/checkout` returned errors or timed out before completing. Customers attempting to place orders during those short windows would have seen failures at the final payment step and, in most cases, succeeded on retry a minute later. No orders were silently lost, no payment was double-charged, and no customer data was exposed or affected.

The impact was concentrated in three spikes, roughly 23 minutes apart. Outside of those windows, checkout performance remained at its normal baseline of around 340 milliseconds per request. In total, the service was meaningfully degraded for approximately three minutes of the 46-minute incident period. Other parts of the platform — browsing, search, cart, and account management — were unaffected throughout.

The root cause was an interaction between our checkout service and a routine background cleanup job. Every 23 minutes, a scheduled task runs to remove expired user sessions from the database, which is a normal part of keeping the system healthy. Over recent months, the volume of sessions to clean up has grown substantially, and the cleanup job had quietly grown from a quick sweep into a multi-second operation that briefly held locks on the same database resources that checkout depends on. While the cleanup was running, new checkout requests could not reach the database fast enough, queued up, and eventually timed out. As soon as the cleanup finished, checkout recovered within seconds. The three spikes in the incident timeline correspond exactly to the three times the cleanup job fired during that window.

We have already shipped the primary fix: the cleanup job now processes expired sessions in small batches with brief pauses between them, so it never holds database resources for more than a few tens of milliseconds at a time. We have also added a dedicated index to make each batch faster, and we have isolated the background job onto its own small database connection pool so it can never compete with live checkout traffic for connections. These changes are live in production and have been validated against synthetic load matching last night's conditions.

Looking ahead, we are adding alerting on background-job duration and on database connection pool saturation, so that a slow-growing job like this one will page us well before it can affect customers. We are also auditing our other scheduled jobs for the same pattern to make sure this class of issue is addressed across the platform, not just for sessions. We are sorry for the disruption to anyone trying to check out during those windows, and we appreciate your patience while we worked through it.

**Internal Retrospective: What Would Have Caught This**

The honest read on last night is that the incident was not caused by a bad change — it was caused by the absence of a change. The session reaper was written two years ago against a table that held a few hundred thousand rows, and at that size a single unbounded delete was a perfectly reasonable design. The job then quietly scaled its way into a problem as the sessions table grew past eight million rows, without ever triggering a code review, a deploy, or any of the checkpoints where we normally catch issues. We have good discipline around reviewing new code; we do not have much discipline around re-reviewing old code whose operating conditions have shifted underneath it. That is the gap worth naming, because it will keep producing incidents of this shape until we close it.

The specific process change that would have caught this is routine observability on our background jobs, treated with the same seriousness as request-path latency. We track p99 on `/api/checkout` closely enough that a 30-millisecond regression gets noticed within a sprint, but we do not track duration, row counts, or lock-wait time on our scheduled jobs at all. A simple dashboard showing reaper duration over the past ninety days would have shown a clear upward curve long before it crossed the threshold where it started interfering with checkout. More broadly, any scheduled job that touches a table also touched by the hot path deserves a standing alert on its duration and on connection pool saturation during its run window — those are the two signals that turned red last night, and neither was being watched.

The reflective piece is that we tend to think of scaling problems as things that happen to new features under load, and we invest our pre-production rigor accordingly: load tests, capacity planning, readiness reviews for launches. Old, quiet, working code gets very little of that attention, and yet it is exactly where slow-moving scaling problems hide, because nothing about the code itself changes to draw the eye. A lightweight quarterly review of our scheduled jobs — what they do, how long they take now versus a quarter ago, what they contend with — would not be expensive, and it would have surfaced this one with room to spare. That is the practice worth adopting out of this incident.
